questions.prompt: The Capulet ball, Mercutio's death, and Tybalt's slaying form this rising action element.
questions.explanation: Complications are events that intensify the central conflict. In Romeo and Juliet 3.1, Mercutio's 'A plague o' both your houses!' and Romeo's revenge killing of Tybalt push the love story toward tragedy. RL.9-10.3 asks students to analyze how complex characters develop over the course of a text; tracking complications is the mechanism. Common Regents task: 'How does an event in lines X-Y advance the plot?'.

questions.prompt: The one-eyed giant Odysseus blinds with a sharpened olive stake bears this name.
questions.explanation: Polyphemus, son of Poseidon, traps Odysseus's crew in his cave in Book 9. Odysseus identifies himself as 'Nobody' (outis), blinds the Cyclops with a heated olive stake, and escapes under sheep. His later boastful revelation of his real name triggers Poseidon's curse, the engine of the wandering. RL.9-10.3 covers cause-effect in plot. Regents tasks on hubris and consequence often draw from this episode.

questions.prompt: Act 3, Scene 1, when Romeo kills Tybalt and is banished, functions as this turning point.
questions.explanation: Climax is the moment of greatest tension where the protagonist's fate pivots. Romeo's killing of Tybalt and instant banishment by the Prince in 3.1 lock the lovers' tragic trajectory; nothing afterward can restore the original possibility of reconciliation. On Common Core RL.9-10.5, students analyze how an author's choices about plot order create effects; pinpointing climax is the central move. Regents short-response often hinges on this.

questions.prompt: The witch who turns Odysseus's men into swine on Aiaia is this enchantress.
questions.explanation: Circe, daughter of Helios, transforms Odysseus's men into pigs in Book 10 until Hermes gives Odysseus the herb moly as antidote. She becomes Odysseus's lover for a year before sending him to consult the dead. RL.9-10.3 covers how characters propel plot. Madeline Miller's 2018 Circe gives her a feminist retelling. Regents source-based arguments on identity and transformation can use Circe as evidence.

questions.prompt: The Prince's closing speech 'A glooming peace this morning with it brings' performs this plot function.
questions.explanation: Resolution (denouement) ties off loose threads after the climax. The Prince's 'glooming peace' and the gold statue promise reconcile the warring houses, restoring civic order at tragic cost. Aristotle called this catastrophe in tragedy. RL.9-10.5 asks how an author structures a text for effect; analyzing how resolutions deliver meaning is a high-value Regents move. Common Part 2 question: 'How does the ending develop a central idea?'.

questions.prompt: Opening scene where Sampson and Gregory brawl with Capulets establishes this in Romeo and Juliet.
questions.explanation: Exposition introduces setting, characters, and conflict. Shakespeare opens Romeo and Juliet with the Chorus's prologue plus the Sampson/Gregory street brawl to plant the Capulet-Montague feud as the engine of every later event. On Grade 9 Common Core RL.9-10.3, students must trace how authors unfold characters and events; identifying exposition is the foundational step. Regents Part 2 passages often open with exposition the writer later complicates.

questions.prompt: Maycomb, Alabama in the 1930s frames every conflict in this Harper Lee novel.
questions.explanation: Harper Lee's 1960 novel uses Depression-era Maycomb as more than backdrop; the town's Jim Crow racial codes, economic stagnation, and gossip networks generate the Tom Robinson trial. RL.9-10.3 expects students to analyze how setting shapes character and theme. Atticus Finch's defense of Robinson is unthinkable outside this specific time-place. Regents Part 3 prompts frequently ask how setting develops a central idea like prejudice or moral courage.

questions.prompt: Ship-Trap Island's jungle and chateau in Connell's short story create this kind of atmosphere.
questions.explanation: Richard Connell's 'The Most Dangerous Game' (1924) opens with sailors' superstition about Ship-Trap Island, then Rainsford finds Zaroff's chateau lit like a 'fairy-tale' but armed with a giant Cossack guard. The jungle setting traps prey. RL.9-10.3 asks how setting propels action and theme; here, the isolation enables Zaroff to hunt humans. Regents short-response: 'How does the setting affect the central idea of survival?'.

questions.prompt: The Salinas River and Soledad ('loneliness' in Spanish) function as this in Of Mice and Men.
questions.explanation: Steinbeck names his town Soledad ('solitude/loneliness') and frames the novella with the Salinas riverbank, the only Eden George and Lennie ever share. The bunkhouse, by contrast, is a transient migrant space. RL.9-10.4 covers analyzing word choice for meaning; Steinbeck's place names enact theme. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the American Dream often draw evidence from setting as symbol.

questions.prompt: The manor farm in this Orwell allegory shifts from Mr. Jones's tyranny to pig-led totalitarianism.
questions.explanation: George Orwell's Animal Farm (1945) uses the farm as setting whose name changes signal political transformation: Manor Farm to Animal Farm to Manor Farm again. The setting traces the Russian Revolution from Tsar to Stalin. RL.9-10.5 asks how structural choices create meaning; the farm's spatial changes (windmill, farmhouse occupancy) embody Orwell's argument about corruption of revolution. Regents argument essays often cite this.

questions.prompt: Mercutio's bawdy wit and skepticism about love contrast with Romeo to highlight him as this.
questions.explanation: A foil is a character whose traits contrast with another to illuminate that character. Mercutio's cynicism about love ('If love be rough with you, be rough with love') foils Romeo's idealism. RL.9-10.3 asks students to analyze how complex characters develop through interaction. Shakespeare uses Mercutio's Queen Mab speech (1.4) to expose Romeo's lovesickness. Regents Part 3: 'How does the relationship between two characters develop a central idea?'.

questions.prompt: Lennie's childlike innocence in Of Mice and Men foils George's worldly weariness, building this theme.
questions.explanation: Steinbeck pairs George Milton (small, sharp, planning) with Lennie Small (huge, gentle, forgetful) to dramatize how rare companionship is in 1930s migrant life. Crooks's later confession 'a guy needs somebody' generalizes the foil's lesson. RL.9-10.2 asks for theme analysis with evidence; the central idea of loneliness emerges through paired contrasts (Curley's wife, Candy, Crooks). Regents argument essays often use this novella as evidence.

questions.prompt: Calm hunter Rainsford and aristocratic killer Zaroff foil each other to develop this central idea.
questions.explanation: Connell sets up Rainsford as a celebrated big-game hunter who initially dismisses animals' fear, then Zaroff reveals himself as a refined cannibal-hunter who quotes Marcus Aurelius. The foil collapses Rainsford's confident hierarchy. RL.9-10.3 asks how characters develop a theme. The closing line, 'He had never slept in a better bed,' inverts moral expectations. Regents Part 3 prompts on ethical complexity favor this story.

questions.prompt: The faithful wife who weaves and unweaves a burial shroud to delay 108 suitors is this queen.
questions.explanation: Penelope's loom trick (weaving Laertes's shroud by day, unraveling it by night) buys her three years against the suitors. Her cunning mirrors Odysseus's polymetis, and her bed test (Book 23) confirms his identity. RL.9-10.3 asks how characters develop themes; Penelope embodies loyalty (kleos) and intelligence. Regents Part 3 prompts on resilience or marriage often use her. Margaret Atwood's Penelopiad updates her perspective.

questions.prompt: Snowball's idealism foils Napoleon's ruthlessness in this Orwell allegory of the Russian Revolution.
questions.explanation: Snowball represents Trotsky (intellectual, plans the windmill, organizes committees) while Napoleon represents Stalin (silent, builds Dogs, expels rivals). Orwell uses the foil to argue that revolutions devour idealists. RL.9-10.3 covers character analysis for theme. The pigs' shifting commandments and Squealer's propaganda extend the contrast. Regents source-based argument essays on power and corruption often draw from this allegorical pairing.

questions.prompt: Poe's titular bird, perched on a bust of Pallas, primarily symbolizes this.
questions.explanation: Edgar Allan Poe in 'The Philosophy of Composition' (1846) explicitly stated the raven symbolizes 'Mournful and Never-ending Remembrance' of his lost Lenore. The bird's single word 'Nevermore' becomes the speaker's torment. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning of words and phrases; analyzing symbolism is core. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice often asks 'What does X symbolize in the passage?' Poe's deliberate symbol design is a teaching staple.

questions.prompt: The bust of this Greek goddess of wisdom that the raven perches upon ironizes the speaker's reason.
questions.explanation: By perching the raven on 'a bust of Pallas just above my chamber door,' Poe juxtaposes the speaker's appeal to classical reason (Athena, goddess of wisdom) against the bird's irrational refrain. The contrast dramatizes the speaker's descent into madness. RL.9-10.4 asks how word choice and allusions create meaning. Regents Part 3 prompts often hinge on allusions; recognizing Athena unlocks Poe's ironic structure.

questions.prompt: Repeated knocks at midnight and the dying embers form this kind of mood-creating element.
questions.explanation: Gothic atmosphere relies on darkness, mystery, decay, and isolation. Poe layers 'midnight dreary,' 'each separate dying ember,' 'silken sad uncertain rustling' to evoke psychological dread. RL.9-10.4 examines connotative word choice; Poe's diction is calibrated for melancholy. The Gothic mode (Walpole, Shelley, Poe) shapes Regents Part 3 source-based prompts on atmosphere and tone. Frankenstein excerpts often pair with The Raven.

questions.prompt: The word the raven repeats, refusing the speaker any hope of reunion with Lenore, is this.
questions.explanation: 'Nevermore' is the poem's haunting refrain, repeated 11 times. Poe in 'Philosophy of Composition' explains he chose it for its long-o sound (most melancholy vowel) and r consonant (most producible). Each repetition deepens the speaker's despair from curiosity to torment. RL.9-10.4 covers analysis of repetition and sound devices. Regents Part 2 frequently asks how a repeated word develops a central idea or tone.

questions.prompt: Frankenstein's monster, abandoned by Victor, and the raven both function as this kind of figure.
questions.explanation: Mary Shelley's Frankenstein (1818) and Poe's poetry share the Romantic-Gothic motif of the doppelganger, the dark double of the protagonist's self. Victor and the Creature mirror each other; the raven externalizes the speaker's grief. RL.9-10.4 asks how figurative language develops complex meaning. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on identity and isolation often draw on doppelganger analysis. AP Lit later builds on this.

questions.prompt: Homer's hero of The Odyssey, king of Ithaca, known by this Greek name.
questions.explanation: Odysseus (Latin: Ulysses) is the Greek king of Ithaca whose ten-year return from Troy frames Homer's epic. Homer's recurring epithet polymetis ('of many devices') captures his defining trait: cunning over brute strength. RL.9-10.3 asks how complex characters develop; Odysseus is the prototype. Regents Part 3 source-based prompts on perseverance, hubris, or identity routinely cite The Odyssey. AP Lit later uses Tennyson's 'Ulysses' as a companion text.

questions.prompt: Friar Laurence's failed letter and Juliet's awakening in the tomb illustrate this kind of irony.
questions.explanation: Dramatic irony occurs when the audience knows something a character does not. Shakespeare lets viewers watch Friar John's quarantine prevent the letter from reaching Romeo, and we see Juliet stir seconds after Romeo drinks poison. RL.9-10.5 requires analyzing structural choices that create suspense or surprise; dramatic irony is the chief device. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments often ask students to discuss how dramatic irony shapes meaning.

questions.prompt: Ithaca, after twenty years of war and wandering, represents this Homeric ideal Odysseus pursues.
questions.explanation: Nostos, the Greek concept of homecoming, is the structural goal of Homer's Odyssey. Ithaca isn't just a destination; it represents identity, family, kingship, and order restored. Penelope's loom, Telemachus's coming-of-age, and the slaughter of the suitors all measure proximity to nostos. RL.9-10.3 emphasizes how setting drives plot. Regents tasks may ask how a journey motif develops a central idea like perseverance or identity.

questions.prompt: Telemachus's youthful uncertainty in The Odyssey foils Odysseus's cunning to develop this theme.
questions.explanation: Books 1-4 of Homer's Odyssey (the Telemachy) trace Telemachus's growth from passive prince to active heir under Athena's guidance, contrasting his uncertainty with his absent father's resourcefulness (polymetis). When father and son finally fight the suitors together (Book 22), the foil resolves. RL.9-10.2 asks for theme tracking; Regents tasks often pair the Telemachy with another text on maturation.

questions.prompt: Homer's repeated descriptive phrases like 'rosy-fingered dawn' function as this oral-tradition device.
questions.explanation: Homeric epithets are formulaic descriptive phrases ('swift-footed Achilles,' 'wine-dark sea,' 'rosy-fingered dawn') that aided oral memory and fit dactylic hexameter meter. Milman Parry's 1930s research proved the oral-formulaic origin. RL.9-10.4 asks how word choice creates meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice often tests recognition of literary terms; epic conventions like epithet, in medias res, and invocation are high-value.

questions.prompt: A central insight about life or human nature that recurs throughout a text is called this.
choices: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.explanation: Theme is not a topic (love, war, freedom) but a complete statement of insight ('Love that defies social rules brings tragedy'). RL.9-10.2 requires students to determine a theme and analyze its development across a text via specific details. Common Core distinguishes theme from summary. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice and Part 3 source-based argument both demand theme identification supported by textual evidence.

questions.prompt: Power corrupts and absolute power corrupts absolutely is the central theme of this Orwell text.
questions.explanation: Animal Farm (1945) traces idealistic revolution descending into Stalinist tyranny: the Seven Commandments shrink to 'All animals are equal, but some are more equal than others.' Orwell's epigraph-like ending shows pigs indistinguishable from humans. RL.9-10.2 asks for theme development through details (changing commandments, the windmill, Boxer's fate). Regents source-based arguments on power, propaganda, or revolution use this text frequently.

questions.prompt: The Most Dangerous Game develops this theme by collapsing Rainsford's certainty about hunters and hunted.
choices: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.correctText: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.explanation: Connell's story opens with Rainsford telling Whitney 'The world is made up of two classes—the hunters and the huntees.' By the end, Rainsford has hunted Zaroff and sleeps in his bed. The theme exposes that moral categories collapse under self-preservation. RL.9-10.2 asks how themes develop through specific details. Regents Part 3 source-based prompts on ethics often pair this story with non-fiction on war or survival.

questions.prompt: Steinbeck's repeated rabbit-and-land dream in Of Mice and Men functions as this device.
choices: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.explanation: A motif is a recurring image, idea, or element that develops theme. Steinbeck recycles George and Lennie's 'we'll have a little place' speech to mark moments of hope, despair, and finally tragedy when George recites it as he shoots Lennie. RL.9-10.2 asks how a theme develops through specific details; motifs are the prime vehicle. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the American Dream typically cite this motif.

questions.prompt: Frankenstein's creature reading Paradise Lost and feeling like Satan develops this theme.
choices: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.explanation: Mary Shelley subtitled Frankenstein 'The Modern Prometheus.' Victor's pursuit of forbidden knowledge mirrors Prometheus's fire, and the Creature reads Paradise Lost, Plutarch, and Werther, gaining self-awareness that becomes torment. RL.9-10.2 covers theme development; Shelley layers Walton's frame, Victor's narrative, and the Creature's testimony to argue knowledge without responsibility destroys. Regents source-based prompts on technology, ethics, and ambition often draw from Frankenstein excerpts.

questions.prompt: Scout Finch as a grown woman recalling her Maycomb childhood uses this point of view.
questions.explanation: Harper Lee uses first-person retrospective narration: adult Scout looks back on her 6-9-year-old experiences, allowing both childlike observation and adult reflection. RL.9-10.6 asks how authors develop point of view to create effects like suspense or sympathy. The double perspective lets Lee dramatize Scout's innocence while moralizing through Atticus. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice frequently tests POV identification; Part 3 essays analyze its effect on theme.

questions.prompt: Nick Carraway in The Great Gatsby and Scout in TKAM share this narrator characteristic.
questions.explanation: A participating narrator is a character inside the story who tells it. Unlike Nick Carraway (who claims observer status while shaping events) or Ishmael in Moby-Dick, Scout is both witness and protagonist. RL.9-10.6 covers point-of-view choices and their effects. The participating narrator creates intimacy and limits the reader to that character's knowledge, often producing dramatic irony. Regents Part 3 prompts often analyze narrator reliability.

questions.prompt: Atticus's instruction to 'climb into his skin and walk around in it' develops this Mockingbird theme.
questions.explanation: Harper Lee uses Atticus Finch's repeated lesson to Scout in Chapter 3 to develop empathy as the novel's moral center. Scout's growth (Boo Radley's porch view in Chapter 31) measures her ability to walk in others' skin. RL.9-10.2 covers theme development across the text. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays frequently use TKAM to evidence themes of empathy, justice, or moral courage. The Common Core exemplar list includes this text.

questions.prompt: The narrator who knows every character's thoughts in The Odyssey and Animal Farm uses this POV.
questions.explanation: Third-person omniscient narration accesses any character's thoughts and roams freely across time and place. Homer narrates from Olympus's view, knowing Athena's plans and the suitors' fates. Orwell's omniscient narrator in Animal Farm appears neutral but cues readers to see propaganda. RL.9-10.6 asks how POV shapes meaning. Regents tasks often contrast omniscient panorama with first-person limitation.

questions.prompt: Edgar Allan Poe's 'Tell-Tale Heart' narrator illustrates this kind of narrator whose grip on truth is questionable.
questions.explanation: An unreliable narrator's credibility is compromised by bias, madness, youth, or moral failing. Poe's narrators (Tell-Tale Heart, Cask of Amontillado) protest sanity while confessing murder. RL.9-10.6 asks students to analyze how POV creates effects; unreliability creates tension between narration and event. Wayne Booth coined the term in 1961. Regents Part 3 prompts on truth, perception, or self-deception often involve unreliable narration.

questions.prompt: Shakespeare writing Romeo's love poetry in this 14-line form with abab cdcd efef gg rhyme reveals high status.
questions.explanation: The Shakespearean sonnet has 14 lines of iambic pentameter rhyming abab cdcd efef gg. Romeo and Juliet's first conversation (1.5.92-105) forms a perfect sonnet between them, marking their love as literary and high-status. The Prologue is also a sonnet. RL.9-10.4 covers structural choices and word patterns; RL.9-10.5 covers form/meaning relationships. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice often tests sonnet recognition; AP Lit later requires deep formal analysis.

questions.prompt: Shakespeare's unrhymed iambic pentameter, used by noble characters in his plays, is this verse form.
questions.explanation: Blank verse is unrhymed iambic pentameter (ten syllables per line, alternating unstressed/stressed). Introduced to English by Henry Howard, Earl of Surrey, Shakespeare uses it for nobility and serious matter; prose for servants and comic scenes. Friar Laurence's herbal speech opens with 'The grey-eyed morn smiles on the frowning night.' RL.9-10.5 asks how form supports meaning. Regents Part 3 prompts can hinge on the prose/verse switch.

questions.prompt: MLK's repeated 'I have a dream' in his 1963 speech demonstrates this rhetorical device.
choices: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.explanation: Anaphora repeats a word or phrase at the start of successive clauses. King's 'I have a dream that one day...' (eight repetitions in his 1963 March on Washington speech) drives momentum and emotion. RI.9-10.6 (informational) and RL.9-10.4 (literary) both cover rhetorical analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments routinely ask how rhetorical devices in MLK, Lincoln, or Douglass speeches develop a central idea. AP Lang heavily tests anaphora.

questions.prompt: Frankenstein's Russian-doll structure of Walton, Victor, and the Creature narrating in turn creates this.
questions.explanation: A frame narrative encloses one story inside another. Mary Shelley's Frankenstein opens with Walton's letters to his sister, then Victor's confession, then the Creature's autobiography, then back out. The structure tests reader sympathies and questions whose version of events is true. RL.9-10.5 asks how authors structure texts for effect. Regents Part 3 source-based prompts on perspective and truth often analyze frame narratives. The Odyssey itself uses frame narration in Books 9-12.

questions.prompt: Connell's narrator using only Rainsford's senses and thoughts during the hunt creates this effect.
questions.explanation: Focalization (Gerard Genette's term) means narration is filtered through one character's consciousness even if technically third-person. Connell limits us to Rainsford's perceptions during the three-day hunt, intensifying suspense. RL.9-10.6 covers POV craft. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test the distinction between omniscient and limited third-person. AP Lit later requires students to name focalization shifts.

questions.prompt: Romeo's 'Juliet is the sun' uses this device of direct comparison without 'like' or 'as.'.
questions.explanation: A metaphor equates two unlike things directly (no 'like' or 'as'). Romeo's balcony speech 'But soft, what light through yonder window breaks? It is the east, and Juliet is the sun' (2.2) uses extended metaphor to convey Juliet's vital, life-giving nature. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests metaphor identification; Part 3 source-based arguments often analyze metaphor's role in developing theme.

questions.prompt: Comparing the raven's eyes to 'a demon's that is dreaming' is this kind of figure using 'like' or 'as.'.
questions.explanation: A simile compares unlike things using 'like' or 'as.' Poe writes 'And his eyes have all the seeming of a demon's that is dreaming' in 'The Raven,' building the gothic atmosphere. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative language. Regents Part 2 routinely tests simile recognition. The distinction from metaphor (no comparison word) is foundational and frequently tested. AP Lit demands students explain why a writer chose simile over metaphor.

questions.prompt: Animal Farm's pigs walking on hind legs and Napoleon ruling form this kind of extended figurative work.
questions.explanation: An allegory is a sustained narrative in which characters, events, and settings systematically represent abstract ideas or historical figures. Animal Farm allegorizes the Russian Revolution: Old Major=Marx/Lenin, Napoleon=Stalin, Snowball=Trotsky, Boxer=working class, Squealer=Pravda propaganda. RL.9-10.4 covers extended figurative meaning. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on power, propaganda, or revolution often demand allegorical reading.

questions.prompt: Animal Farm's pigs speaking, planning, and writing commandments illustrates this device.
questions.explanation: Personification gives human qualities to non-human entities. Orwell pushes personification into full anthropomorphism: pigs speak, plan, and finally walk upright. The device lets Orwell critique humans by displacing them onto animals. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative language. Regents Part 3 prompts often ask how a non-human element (the green light in Gatsby, the conch in Lord of the Flies) is personified to develop theme.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'sweltering with the heat of injustice' overstates for effect, demonstrating this device.
questions.explanation: Hyperbole is intentional exaggeration for emphasis or effect. MLK's 'I Have a Dream' uses 'sweltering with the heat of injustice' and 'mountain of despair' to magnify the urgency of civil rights. RL.9-10.4 and RI.9-10.6 both cover rhetorical exaggeration. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on civil rights speeches often analyze how hyperbole intensifies argumentative force. AP Lang students must distinguish hyperbole from understatement (litotes).

questions.prompt: When Atticus references 'Hitlerism' in TKAM, he uses this kind of brief reference.
questions.explanation: An allusion is a brief reference to a person, place, event, or text the audience is expected to recognize. Atticus's classroom scene reference to Hitler positions Maycomb's racism inside a contemporary global injustice. RL.9-10.4 covers analyzing word choices including allusions. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments frequently test allusion recognition (Biblical, mythological, historical). King's letter from Birmingham Jail layers Socrates, Aquinas, and the Apostle Paul.

questions.prompt: Old Major's speech echoing this 1848 political document grounds Animal Farm's allegory.
questions.explanation: Old Major's barn speech ('Man is the only real enemy we have. Remove Man from the scene') paraphrases Marx and Engels's Communist Manifesto (1848). Orwell's allegory depends on readers recognizing this allusion to understand the Russian Revolution parallel. RL.9-10.4 covers allusion analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments often pair Animal Farm with primary historical documents. Common Core appendices encourage paired literary-informational reading.

questions.prompt: The Creature in Frankenstein comparing himself to 'the fallen angel' alludes to this Milton epic.
questions.explanation: The Creature finds Milton's Paradise Lost (1667) and identifies with Satan, the fallen angel cast out by his Creator. Mary Shelley uses the epigraph from Paradise Lost on her title page: 'Did I request thee, Maker, from my clay / To mould me Man?' RL.9-10.4 covers allusion. The Creature's literary education (Plutarch, Werther, Milton) is central to his moral arc. Regents Part 3 prompts on identity often draw on Frankenstein's allusive richness.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'I have a dream' speech alludes to this Lincoln 1863 address with 'Five score years ago.'.
questions.explanation: King opens his 1963 March on Washington speech with 'Five score years ago, a great American, in whose symbolic shadow we stand today, signed the Emancipation Proclamation.' The 'five score' parallels Lincoln's famous 'four score and seven years ago' from the Gettysburg Address (1863), positioning King in Lincoln's prophetic tradition. RI.9-10.6 and RL.9-10.4 both cover allusion. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on civil rights speeches frequently test this allusion chain.

questions.prompt: When 'all animals are equal, but some are more equal than others' inverts this Jefferson founding phrase.
questions.explanation: Orwell's final Seven Commandment ('All animals are equal, but some are more equal than others') ironically inverts Jefferson's Declaration of Independence (1776) preamble 'all men are created equal.' The allusion exposes how revolutionary rhetoric is twisted to justify hierarchy. RL.9-10.4 covers allusion as a complex meaning-making device. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on equality and power routinely test this kind of ironic intertextuality.

questions.prompt: The single most important point a non-fiction text makes is this.
questions.explanation: Main idea (central idea) is the overarching point of an informational text, supported by details, examples, and evidence. RI.9-10.2 explicitly asks students to determine a central idea and analyze its development. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely opens with 'Which sentence best states the central idea?' Part 3 source-based argument essays require synthesizing central ideas across multiple texts. Distinct from a topic (one or two words); a main idea is a complete sentence.

questions.prompt: The author's main position or arguable assertion in an argumentative text is this.
questions.explanation: A claim is the author's arguable position. RI.9-10.6 examines point of view and purpose; RI.9-10.8 analyzes claims. Toulmin's model (claim, data, warrant, backing, rebuttal, qualifier) shapes argument analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument requires identifying claims across multiple sources and forming the student's own claim. AP Lang's argument essay rubric weighs claim quality heavily. Distinct from a topic; a claim must be debatable.

questions.prompt: The opposing view the writer must acknowledge and respond to is this in argumentative writing.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is the opposing position a writer addresses to strengthen the main claim. W.9-10.1b requires students to 'develop claim(s) and counterclaims fairly, supplying evidence for each.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly evaluate counterclaim acknowledgment. The strongest arguments concede something to the counterclaim ('While critics rightly note X, the evidence still favors Y'). AP Lang argument essays heavily reward genuine counterclaim engagement.

questions.prompt: Specific facts, examples, statistics, and quotations that prove a main idea are called these.
choices: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.explanation: Supporting details are the specific facts, examples, anecdotes, statistics, and quotations that develop and prove a main idea. RI.9-10.1 requires citing strong and thorough textual evidence; RI.9-10.2 analyzes how details develop a central idea. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests detail-main-idea relationships ('Which detail best supports the central idea?'). Part 3 source-based argument essays demand that students embed specific details, not paraphrase generically.

questions.prompt: When Frederick Douglass describes his struggle to learn to read, he uses this kind of evidence.
questions.explanation: Personal anecdote (autobiographical evidence) uses the writer's own experience as proof. Douglass's Narrative (1845) recounts how Mrs. Auld first taught him letters, then Mr. Auld forbade it, revealing literacy's threat to slavery. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric; RI.9-10.8 analyzes argument. The Common Core informational text list includes Douglass. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on education, freedom, or self-making frequently test how anecdote functions as evidence.

questions.prompt: An assertion that something is true or false, based on verifiable evidence, is this claim type.
questions.explanation: A claim of fact asserts that something is, was, or will be true based on evidence ('Long Island temperatures have risen 2 degrees since 1970'). It differs from a claim of value (good/bad) or a claim of policy (should/should not). RI.9-10.8 covers claim evaluation. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often combine all three claim types; identifying which is which unlocks the analysis. Stephen Toulmin's framework underlies AP Lang training.

questions.prompt: An assertion that something is good, bad, just, unjust, ethical, or unethical is this claim type.
questions.explanation: A claim of value judges merit or worth, ethics or aesthetics ('Civil disobedience is morally justified when laws are unjust'). MLK's letter argues a claim of value about just versus unjust laws, citing Aquinas. RI.9-10.8 distinguishes claim types. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays frequently pose value questions (Is X worth the cost? Should Y be allowed?). Students must defend a value claim with evidence and reasoning.

questions.prompt: The reasoning that connects a piece of evidence to a claim is called this in Toulmin's argument model.
questions.explanation: In Stephen Toulmin's argument model, a warrant is the underlying assumption or reasoning that connects data (evidence) to a claim. If the claim is 'cars should be electric' and the data is 'electric cars emit less carbon,' the warrant is 'we should reduce carbon emissions.' RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument writing heavily reward explicit warrants ('this matters because...'). Implicit warrants weaken arguments.

questions.prompt: An argument is strong only if its evidence is both sufficient in quantity and this in quality.
questions.explanation: Evidence quality has two dimensions: sufficiency (enough of it) and relevance (it actually proves the claim). RI.9-10.8 explicitly asks students to delineate and evaluate the argument and specific claims in a text, including assessing whether reasoning is valid and evidence is relevant and sufficient. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require evaluating evidence quality, not just citing it. AP Lang later builds this into rhetorical analysis essays.

questions.prompt: A claim of cause-and-effect, asserting one thing produces another, is this claim type.
questions.explanation: A claim of cause asserts that X causes Y (or Y is the effect of X). Causal claims require evidence beyond correlation: temporal sequence, mechanism, ruling out alternative causes. RI.9-10.8 evaluates reasoning quality. Common logical fallacies (post hoc ergo propter hoc, correlation-as-causation) trap weak causal arguments. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on social problems often rest on causal claims. AP Lang stamps out fallacies in argument analysis.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's appeal to logic, reasoning, and evidence is called this.
questions.explanation: Logos is Aristotle's appeal to logic and reason, supported by facts, statistics, examples, and valid inference. From Rhetoric, Aristotle's three artistic appeals are ethos (character), pathos (emotion), and logos (logic). RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis demand identification of logos: Was the writer's evidence empirical? Was the syllogism valid? Quantitative data, expert testimony, and historical precedent all serve logos.

questions.prompt: MLK appealing to readers' sense of fairness and grief over injustice draws on this Aristotelian appeal.
questions.explanation: Pathos is Aristotle's appeal to emotion, mobilizing audience feelings of pity, anger, fear, hope, or pride. King's Birmingham letter describes 'when you have seen vicious mobs lynch your mothers and fathers' to evoke moral outrage. RI.9-10.6 examines rhetorical choices. Regents Part 3 essays often analyze how pathos drives a speech (King, Lincoln, Douglass). AP Lang rhetorical analysis requires precise emotion-naming, not just 'this is emotional.'.

questions.prompt: When a writer briefly grants a point of the opposing view before refuting it, this rhetorical move occurs.
questions.explanation: Concession acknowledges the merit of an opposing point before reasserting the writer's position. King concedes in Birmingham letter that some laws should be obeyed but argues unjust laws must be resisted. W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim handling, of which concession is a key move. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments and AP Lang argument essays reward strategic concession because it builds ethos by showing fairness.

questions.prompt: The speaker's credibility, character, and authority, as Aristotle named it, is this appeal.
questions.explanation: Ethos is Aristotle's appeal based on the speaker's character, credibility, and trustworthiness. MLK builds ethos by signing as 'Pastor' and quoting Augustine, Aquinas, and Buber, demonstrating theological authority. Douglass's autobiography builds ethos through firsthand experience of slavery. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis routinely test ethos identification and effect.

questions.prompt: Linking 'I have a dream' to 'four score and seven years ago' is a rhetorical appeal to this.
questions.explanation: Appeals to shared history and tradition are rhetorical strategies layered onto ethos/pathos. MLK opens with 'five score years ago' to echo Lincoln, positioning his speech as the next chapter of American freedom. Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852) uses tradition ironically against itself. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical strategy. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on national identity routinely require tradition-appeal analysis.

questions.prompt: An eyewitness account or original document from the time of an event is this kind of source.
questions.explanation: A primary source originates from the time and place of the event: diaries, letters, speeches, original photographs, interviews, original government documents. Frederick Douglass's Narrative is a primary source on slavery. RI.9-10.1 requires citing strong textual evidence; understanding primary vs secondary unlocks source analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays mix primary and secondary; identification helps evaluate reliability. AP US History training also emphasizes this.

questions.prompt: A textbook or scholar's analysis written after an event, based on primary sources, is this.
questions.explanation: A secondary source analyzes, interprets, or reports on primary sources after the fact: textbooks, scholarly articles, biographies, documentaries. A historian writing about MLK uses King's primary speeches and creates a secondary work. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view; RI.9-10.7 analyzes accounts across mediums. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays mix source types; evaluating each requires recognizing the distinction. AP Lang argument essays require source-type awareness.

questions.prompt: Three things you check on a source: author's credentials, publication, and this third C.
questions.explanation: The CRAAP test (Currency, Relevance, Authority, Accuracy, Purpose) is a standard source-evaluation tool. Currency matters because outdated data on rapidly changing topics (technology, climate, public health) misleads. RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating reasoning and evidence. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes plant outdated statistics for students to flag. AP Lang demands source vetting in argument and synthesis essays. Common Core's research strand (W.9-10.8) explicitly covers source evaluation.

questions.prompt: A logical error like 'either we ban guns or our children die' commits this specific fallacy.
questions.explanation: False dilemma (false dichotomy) presents only two options when more exist. RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating reasoning and identifying false statements or fallacious reasoning. Other common fallacies: ad hominem (attacking the person), straw man (misrepresenting opposing view), slippery slope, hasty generalization, appeal to authority, post hoc, circular reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments often plant a fallacy in one source for students to detect. AP Lang rigorously tests fallacy identification.

questions.prompt: A claim that 'studies show' without identifying which studies commits this evidence weakness.
questions.explanation: Unverifiable evidence (vague attribution to 'studies' or 'experts' without naming them) prevents readers from checking sources. RI.9-10.8 requires evaluating evidence quality. The phrase 'studies show' is a red flag in argument analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments may include a source guilty of this weakness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis trains students to spot unverifiable evidence as a deliberate or accidental rhetorical move.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' replies to white clergy with this kind of structured argument.
questions.explanation: Refutation is the part of an argument that addresses and disproves opposing views. King's 1963 letter responds point-by-point to eight Alabama clergymen who called his protests 'unwise and untimely,' refuting their charges of outsider status, lawbreaking, and impatience. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and rhetoric; RI.9-10.8 covers evaluation of argument. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on civil rights regularly use this letter; recognizing refutation structure unlocks the analysis.

questions.prompt: A claim asserting a specific course of action 'should' or 'must' be taken is this type.
questions.explanation: A claim of policy advocates a specific action ('Schools should adopt later start times'). Policy claims typically build on fact claims (problem exists) and value claims (problem is bad) to argue what should be done. RI.9-10.8 covers argument evaluation. Regents Part 3 source-based argument prompts often pose policy questions. AP Lang argument essays frequently require policy positions. The structure problem-cause-solution organizes most policy arguments.

questions.prompt: A source whose hidden financial or political interest shapes its content has this problem.
questions.explanation: Bias is a source's unstated preference, often shaped by funding, political affiliation, or personal investment. A tobacco-company study on smoking's health effects has structural bias. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and purpose; RI.9-10.8 evaluates argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes pair a clearly biased source with a more neutral one to test student detection. AP Lang's argument and synthesis essays heavily reward bias-aware source treatment.

questions.prompt: The final element in argumentative writing that restates the position and shows broader significance is this.
questions.explanation: A conclusion restates the claim, summarizes key reasons, and shows broader implications ('so what'). W.9-10.1e requires students to 'provide a concluding statement or section that follows from and supports the argument presented.' Weak conclusions just paraphrase the thesis; strong ones answer 'why does this matter?' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays demand effective conclusions; AP Lang argument essays explicitly reward strong final paragraphs.

questions.prompt: Structuring an essay 'On one hand... On the other hand...' creates this kind of argumentative organization.
questions.explanation: Point-counterpoint structure alternates claims and counterclaims, then synthesizes. Distinct from block organization (all claims first, then all counterclaims). W.9-10.1a covers introducing and distinguishing claim from counterclaim; W.9-10.1c covers using transitions to clarify relationships. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward clear organizational structure. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score organization. Knowing your structural options lets you choose strategically.

questions.prompt: A speaker expressing barely controlled outrage at injustice has this tone.
questions.explanation: Indignant tone conveys righteous anger at wrongdoing or injustice. Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852) reaches indignant peaks: 'This Fourth of July is yours, not mine.' RI.9-10.4 examines word choice and tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests precise tone naming. AP Lang requires students to distinguish indignant (righteous anger) from angry (unspecified rage) or bitter (resentful)—a precise tone vocabulary unlocks rhetorical analysis.

questions.prompt: Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' to eat Irish babies demonstrates this tone of biting irony.
questions.explanation: Satirical tone uses irony, exaggeration, and ridicule to expose folly or vice. Jonathan Swift's 'A Modest Proposal' (1729) deadpan-suggests Irish parents sell their babies as food to wealthy English, satirizing English indifference to Irish famine. RI.9-10.4 covers tone. Orwell's Animal Farm uses satirical narration. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often include satirical passages students must recognize. AP Lang rhetorical analysis requires satire identification.

questions.prompt: Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (November 1863) is famously this brief, just 272 words.
questions.explanation: Lincoln's Gettysburg Address ran approximately 272 words and lasted about two minutes, framed by Edward Everett's two-hour oration that nobody remembers. Compression is itself a rhetorical strategy. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical choices. Common Core lists the Gettysburg Address among the founding documents for Grades 9-12 informational study. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on memory, sacrifice, or democracy often use this text. AP Lang heavily features it.

questions.prompt: Atticus's even-handed, calm courtroom voice in TKAM exemplifies this kind of tone.
questions.explanation: Measured tone is deliberate, calm, and balanced, neither inflamed nor cold. Atticus's closing argument to the jury in TKAM maintains measured pleas for justice despite the case's emotional stakes. RI.9-10.4 covers tone analysis. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests measured versus passionate, restrained versus emphatic. AP Lang students must distinguish measured from indifferent (uninvested) and from impassioned (heightened emotion).

questions.prompt: An argument that grants the reader's likely position before contesting it builds this rhetorical quality.
questions.explanation: Goodwill (Aristotle's eunoia) is the rhetorical impression that the writer cares about the audience's wellbeing. Concession to opposing views, fair tone, and audience-aware diction build goodwill. Strong arguments earn the reader's trust before pressing claims. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward writing that builds goodwill. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score audience awareness. Often invisible to weak readers but powerful in skilled hands.

questions.prompt: MLK's measured patience in 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' best describes this tone.
questions.explanation: Resolute tone shows firm, unwavering commitment without anger or whimsy. King's Birmingham letter (1963) responds to charges of impatience with measured insistence that justice cannot wait, while maintaining moral authority. RI.9-10.4 covers analysis of word meaning and tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice frequently tests tone identification using precise vocabulary (resolute, indignant, conciliatory, defiant). AP Lang demands students name tone with specificity, not just 'serious' or 'sad.'.

questions.prompt: Frederick Douglass writing his 1845 Narrative to expose slavery's horrors aims primarily to do this.
questions.explanation: Author's purpose can be to inform, persuade, entertain, or express. Frederick Douglass's Narrative (1845) is autobiographical and informative, but its primary purpose is persuasive: to abolish slavery by making readers see its evils through Douglass's experience. RI.9-10.6 explicitly examines purpose. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests purpose identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must explain how the writer's choices advance the specific purpose.

questions.prompt: An article explaining how vaccines work, written for general readers, has this primary purpose.
questions.explanation: Informational/expository purpose is to explain or convey knowledge clearly without arguing a position. Science writing, encyclopedia entries, technical manuals, and most news reporting are primarily informational. RI.9-10.6 covers purpose. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests purpose categorization. AP Lang requires students to distinguish informational from argumentative (where a position is taken) and from expressive (where the writer's emotions or experience drive the text).

questions.prompt: MLK's repeated questions 'Why direct action? Why sit-ins, marches, and so forth?' serve this rhetorical purpose.
questions.explanation: King anticipates objections by posing the clergy's likely questions before answering them. This rhetorical move builds ethos (shows fairness) and demonstrates argument mastery. W.9-10.1b explicitly requires anticipating counterclaims. RI.9-10.6 examines rhetorical purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward this move. AP Lang argument essays rate concession-and-refutation highly. The skill links reading and writing strands together.

questions.prompt: Joan Didion's personal essays meditating on California and grief illustrate this purpose.
questions.explanation: Expressive purpose centers the writer's own perspective, feelings, or experience, often in personal essays, memoirs, or literary journalism. Joan Didion's 'The Year of Magical Thinking' (2005) and 'Slouching Towards Bethlehem' (1968) blend reportage with personal reflection. RI.9-10.6 covers purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument prompts occasionally include literary nonfiction. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays often draw on expressive writing as evidence.

questions.prompt: King's letter from Birmingham Jail was written on margins of this material smuggled into his cell.
questions.explanation: King wrote his letter on margins of newspapers and scraps of paper smuggled into his Birmingham cell after his April 1963 arrest. The physical conditions of composition (no library access, no typewriter) make King's theological and historical command of citation more remarkable. RI.9-10.6 covers context shaping rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes test contextual awareness. AP Lang rewards essays that note rhetorical situation factors.

questions.prompt: Lincoln's 'government of the people, by the people, for the people' uses this rhetorical pattern.
questions.explanation: Tricolon is a series of three parallel words, phrases, or clauses. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address closes with 'of the people, by the people, for the people.' The rhythm and completeness of three feels both balanced and emphatic. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical choices. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often analyze tricolons in speeches (Caesar's 'veni, vidi, vici'; FDR's 'fear, hope, faith'). AP Lang scoring rewards naming the device, not just noticing it.

questions.prompt: JFK's 'Ask not what your country can do for you—ask what you can do for your country' uses this.
questions.explanation: Chiasmus reverses the order of words in parallel clauses (ABBA pattern). JFK's 1961 inaugural address features the famous 'Ask not what your country can do for you—ask what you can do for your country.' Mae West: 'It's not the men in my life, it's the life in my men.' RI.9-10.6 covers advanced rhetorical figures. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on civic identity sometimes use JFK. AP Lang demands precise device-naming (chiasmus vs antimetabole).

questions.prompt: The writer's use of 'you' to address readers directly creates this rhetorical relationship.
questions.explanation: Second-person address ('you' direct address) creates intimacy and pulls the reader into the argument. Persuasive writing often uses 'you' to build relationship. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetorical strategy. James Baldwin's 'My Dungeon Shook' (1963) addresses his nephew directly, modeling how second-person address makes general arguments personal. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes feature second-person texts. AP Lang demands students analyze pronoun choice.

questions.prompt: Mark Antony's 'Brutus is an honourable man' repeated with growing irony uses this rhetorical device.
questions.explanation: Verbal irony means the opposite of what is said. Antony's funeral oration in Julius Caesar 3.2 repeats 'Brutus is an honourable man' with rising sarcasm until the crowd hears it as accusation. RI.9-10.6 and RL.9-10.4 both cover irony. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests irony types: verbal (saying opposite), situational (event-outcome mismatch), dramatic (audience knows what character does not). AP Lang rewards precise irony analysis.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'sweltering heat of injustice' and 'oasis of freedom' use this trope of comparison.
questions.explanation: Metaphor compresses comparison without 'like' or 'as.' MLK's 1963 speech threads metaphors: 'the heat of injustice,' 'an oasis of freedom and justice,' 'a check stamped insufficient funds.' RI.9-10.4 and L.9-10.5 both cover figurative language. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often analyze how metaphor advances claim. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise metaphor-effect analysis: not 'this metaphor is powerful' but 'this metaphor positions X as Y, evoking Z.'.

questions.prompt: MLK delivered 'I Have a Dream' at this 1963 event, attended by 250,000 people.
questions.explanation: The March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom (August 28, 1963) drew 250,000 to the Lincoln Memorial. King's 'I Have a Dream' speech anchored the event and pressured passage of the 1964 Civil Rights Act. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view shaped by context; RI.9-10.9 analyzes documents of historical/literary significance. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on civil rights often require historical contextualization.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'we will not be satisfied until justice rolls down like waters' uses this device.
questions.explanation: King quotes Amos 5:24 ('Let justice roll down like waters'), one of many Biblical allusions in his speeches that establish ethos with his Black church audience and white religious moderates. RI.9-10.4 covers allusion as word choice. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test allusion recognition. AP Lang demands students show how the allusion creates effect (ethos with religious audience, prophetic framing of civil rights). Other King allusions: Isaiah, Galatians.

questions.prompt: Patrick Henry's 'Give me liberty or give me death' speech has this primary purpose.
choices: Predator and prey are unstable categories
questions.explanation: Motivating to action is a subtype of persuasion focused on producing behavior, not just changing beliefs. Patrick Henry's 1775 speech aimed to rally the Virginia Convention to take up arms; MLK's 'I Have a Dream' rallied civil rights action. RI.9-10.6 covers purpose. W.9-10.1e covers concluding statements that follow from argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often analyze speeches; AP Lang rhetorical analysis demands recognizing the action-call purpose.

questions.prompt: Speech analysis acronym SOAPSTone stands for Speaker, Occasion, Audience, Purpose, Subject, and this.
questions.explanation: SOAPSTone (Speaker, Occasion, Audience, Purpose, Subject, Tone) is a College Board-developed rhetorical analysis tool. It forces students to map every element of the rhetorical situation before analyzing devices. RI.9-10.6 explicitly covers point of view and purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require SOAPSTone thinking. AP Lang training builds rhetorical analysis on this framework. Knowing SOAPSTone unlocks any speech or essay.

questions.prompt: When a writer's claim is openly stated rather than implied, the stance is described as this.
questions.explanation: An explicit stance is openly stated; an implicit stance is suggested through tone, evidence selection, and word choice. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. Skilled writers may take an implicit stance (Animal Farm's anti-Stalinism) or an explicit one (King's anti-segregation). Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests stance identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays often examine implicit stances in nonfiction prose where the writer doesn't directly state the claim.

questions.prompt: A writer who treats both sides fairly without taking a position has this stance.
questions.explanation: Objective stance presents information without favoring a side. Encyclopedic and most news writing aim for objectivity. RI.9-10.6 covers stance. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests stance categorization. AP Lang requires distinguishing objective (neutral) from subjective (personal-view-laden) writing. True objectivity is contested; writers' choices about what to include shape stance even without explicit position. The distinction is foundational for source evaluation.

questions.prompt: A writer using 'I,' personal anecdote, and emotional language adopts this stance.
questions.explanation: Subjective stance foregrounds the writer's personal perspective, often through first-person narration, anecdote, and emotionally charged language. Joan Didion, James Baldwin, and Frederick Douglass write subjectively while making larger arguments. RI.9-10.6 covers stance. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pair subjective and objective sources. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays must integrate both stances strategically; rhetorical analysis must name when and why a writer is subjective.

questions.prompt: An author who treats a serious topic with affected lightness or dark humor uses this tone.
questions.explanation: Sardonic tone combines scorn with bitter humor; it mocks while smiling. Orwell's 1984 narrator describing Big Brother is sardonic. Mark Twain's Huckleberry Finn often sardonically narrates the 'sivilizing' efforts of polite society. RI.9-10.4 covers tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests advanced tone vocabulary. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays explicitly reward precise tone vocabulary (sardonic, wry, acerbic, jocular). Beyond 'serious' or 'funny.'.

questions.prompt: The triangular relationship of writer, audience, and subject is called the rhetorical this.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical triangle (Aristotle) connects writer/speaker (ethos), audience (pathos), and subject (logos). Effective rhetoric balances all three. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pose questions about audience or speaker stance. AP Lang's rhetorical analysis essay requires mapping the triangle: who is speaking, to whom, about what, and how the writer's choices serve all three points.

questions.prompt: An arguable, specific, defensible statement of the writer's position is called this.
questions.explanation: A thesis is the writer's specific, arguable, defensible position. W.9-10.1a requires introducing precise claims. Strong theses are debatable (not just factual), specific (not vague), and forecast organization. Weak: 'Romeo and Juliet is interesting.' Strong: 'Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet uses dramatic irony to argue that youthful impulsiveness undoes even the best intentions.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score thesis quality heavily. AP Lang argument essays require thesis identification.

questions.prompt: A thesis must be this quality: capable of being argued, with reasonable opposing views possible.
questions.explanation: Arguability distinguishes a thesis from a topic statement. 'World War II started in 1939' is factual, not arguable. 'World War II's primary cause was the Treaty of Versailles, not Hitler's personal ambition' is arguable. W.9-10.1a requires writers to introduce 'precise claim(s).' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward arguable theses. AP Lang's argument essay rubric distinguishes defensible thesis (1 pt) from no thesis (0 pts).

questions.prompt: Restating an author's idea in your own words while maintaining accuracy is this.
questions.explanation: Paraphrase restates an author's idea in your own words, sentence-by-sentence, with attribution. Distinct from summary (which compresses) and direct quotation (which preserves wording). W.9-10.8 explicitly covers paraphrasing source material. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept paraphrase with attribution; AP Lang argument essays favor strategic mixing of quotation and paraphrase. Plagiarism risk: paraphrasing without attribution still counts as plagiarism.

questions.prompt: A thesis that forecasts the essay's three main supporting points uses this structure.
questions.explanation: A tripartite thesis forecasts three main supports ('Shakespeare uses dramatic irony, foreshadowing, and parallel structure to develop tragedy'). The structure aids organization but can feel formulaic; advanced writers may use it strategically and then abandon it. W.9-10.1a covers claim introduction. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often reward tripartite organization. AP Lang argument essays accept more sophisticated thesis structures.

questions.prompt: A weak thesis that simply restates the prompt or asserts something obvious has this flaw.
questions.explanation: Vague theses ('This essay will discuss themes in TKAM') fail because they neither argue nor specify. Strong theses name the text, the specific claim, and often the mechanism. W.9-10.1a explicitly requires 'precise claim(s).' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays penalize thesis vagueness. AP Lang's argument essay rubric awards thesis points only for defensible, specific claims. Students should ask: would anyone disagree with my thesis? If not, it's not a thesis.

questions.prompt: Addressing 'they say' before 'I say' in an argument creates this kind of structure.
questions.explanation: Gerald Graff and Cathy Birkenstein's 'They Say / I Say' framework structures arguments as a conversation: state opposing views, then respond. W.9-10.1b requires developing claims AND counterclaims 'fairly.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly require counterclaim engagement. AP Lang argument essays score counterargument quality. The structure builds ethos by showing the writer has considered the other side, then rebuts.

questions.prompt: When a writer briefly acknowledges that the opposing view has some validity, this rhetorical move occurs.
questions.explanation: Concession briefly grants the opposing view's merit before reasserting the writer's position. 'While critics rightly note X, the evidence still favors Y.' Concession builds ethos (fairness) without surrendering the argument. W.9-10.1b explicitly requires fair counterclaim development; concession is a primary move. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays heavily reward strategic concession. Refusing concession looks defensive and weakens credibility.

questions.prompt: The part of an argument that directly disproves an opposing view is this.
questions.explanation: Refutation disproves the opposing view through evidence, logical analysis, or exposing fallacies. W.9-10.1b covers counterclaim development; refutation is the final step after acknowledgment and concession. King's Birmingham letter refutes the clergy's charges of 'unwise and untimely' by exposing the false dichotomy between order and justice. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward refutation. AP Lang argument essays rate refutation quality as part of overall sophistication.

questions.prompt: Inserting 'although' or 'despite' before stating the claim creates this kind of sophisticated thesis.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim-acknowledging thesis begins with 'Although critics argue X...' or 'Despite Y...' before stating the writer's position. The structure demonstrates argument awareness and sophistication. Example: 'Although some readers view Atticus as a savior figure, Lee's portrayal more honestly positions him as a flawed but principled lawyer.' W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward this structure.

questions.prompt: When a writer concedes one point and then asserts an even stronger position, this rhetorical effect emerges.
questions.explanation: Strategic concession-then-counter strengthens ethos: the writer appears fair, then more authoritative. 'Some say technology isolates teens, and certainly excessive screen time does pose risks—but research shows social media also enables marginalized teens to find community impossible offline.' W.9-10.1b explicitly covers fair counterclaim handling. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward ethos-building moves. Aristotle's rhetorical triangle requires this attention to character.

questions.prompt: Reproducing an author's exact words inside quotation marks creates this kind of evidence use.
questions.explanation: Direct quotation reproduces exact words inside quotation marks with attribution. W.9-10.1b requires supporting claims with relevant evidence. W.9-10.8 covers gathering and integrating information from sources. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require direct quotation from numbered sources. AP Lang argument essays reward strategic quotation. Direct quotation matters when the author's exact phrasing is significant; paraphrase matters when the idea, not wording, is the point.

questions.prompt: Briefly introducing a quotation with context like 'Lincoln, addressing a divided nation, declared' uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Signal phrases (a.k.a. tags or attributions) introduce quotations with context: 'As King argues...' 'According to Source 2...' 'Douglass, writing from personal experience, observes...' W.9-10.1c covers cohesion and clear relationships. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly require source attribution; failure to attribute drops a score band. AP Lang argument essays favor varied signal phrases over repetitive 'X says.' Effective signal phrases include the writer's authority or perspective.

questions.prompt: Following a quotation with 1-2 sentences explaining its meaning and relevance creates this writing move.
questions.explanation: Commentary (analysis) follows evidence with the writer's interpretation of its meaning and relevance to the claim. The PEEL paragraph structure (Point, Evidence, Explanation, Link) bakes commentary in. W.9-10.1b requires supplying evidence AND reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly require analysis, not just evidence dump. AP Lang argument essays score 'sophistication' partly through commentary depth. Strong commentary explains why the evidence matters, not just what it says.

questions.prompt: Drawing a broad conclusion from a single example or insufficient evidence commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Hasty generalization draws a broad conclusion from insufficient evidence. 'My grandfather smoked and lived to 90, so smoking doesn't harm health.' W.9-10.1b requires sufficient evidence. RI.9-10.8 evaluates evidence quality. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays penalize this in student writing and test recognition in sources. AP Lang argument essays explicitly require evidence-claim proportion. One example rarely supports a universal claim.

questions.prompt: Arguing 'X must be true because no one has proven it false' commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Appeal to ignorance (argumentum ad ignorantiam) claims something is true because it hasn't been proven false (or vice versa). 'No one has proven aliens don't exist, so they must.' The fallacy reverses burden of proof. RI.9-10.8 evaluates argument soundness. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test reasoning quality. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score logical reasoning. Strong arguments establish claims with positive evidence, not absence of refutation.

questions.prompt: Words like 'furthermore,' 'moreover,' and 'additionally' signal this kind of relationship.
questions.explanation: Addition transitions ('furthermore,' 'moreover,' 'additionally,' 'in addition,' 'also,' 'similarly') signal that the next idea adds support or evidence to the current argument. W.9-10.1c requires linking major sections with transitions. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score cohesion; transitions are the primary cohesion mechanism. AP Lang argument essays reward varied, purposeful transitions. Avoid overusing 'also' or starting every paragraph with 'another reason'—boring transitions signal weak craft.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'however,' 'on the contrary,' and 'in contrast' signal this kind of relationship.
questions.explanation: Contrast transitions ('however,' 'on the other hand,' 'on the contrary,' 'nevertheless,' 'whereas,' 'although,' 'while,' 'in contrast') signal opposition or unexpected shift. They're essential for counterargument structure. W.9-10.1c covers transition use to clarify claim-counterclaim relationships. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays heavily use contrast transitions. AP Lang argument essays demand strategic contrast handling: not just 'however,' but precise differentiation.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'therefore,' 'thus,' 'consequently,' and 'as a result' signal this relationship.
questions.explanation: Cause-effect transitions ('therefore,' 'thus,' 'consequently,' 'as a result,' 'hence,' 'so,' 'because of this,' 'this means that') signal logical or causal relationships. They're critical in argument writing because they show how evidence supports claims. W.9-10.1c covers transitions for cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward causal connectors. AP Lang argument essays score reasoning quality; explicit causal transitions show the warrant.

questions.prompt: Repeating key terms across paragraphs to build connection is this cohesion technique.
questions.explanation: Lexical chains weave repeated key terms and their synonyms across paragraphs to build textual cohesion. An essay on Animal Farm's allegory might chain 'allegory,' 'allegorical,' 'symbol,' 'represents,' 'embodies,' 'stands for.' W.9-10.1c covers cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score writing quality including cohesion. AP Lang argument essays reward sophisticated lexical handling. Halliday and Hasan's 1976 cohesion theory underlies this analysis.

questions.prompt: When each paragraph's first sentence connects to the previous paragraph's last sentence, this occurs.
questions.explanation: Linked transitions (bridge sentences) create flow by referencing the previous paragraph at the start of a new one. 'Beyond Atticus's courtroom decency, Lee also positions Calpurnia as a model of bicultural fluency.' W.9-10.1c covers paragraph-level cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward strong paragraph transitions. AP Lang argument essays score essays as wholes; paragraph linkage is a sophistication marker. Effective bridges acknowledge what came before before moving forward.

questions.prompt: Attacking the person rather than their argument commits this logical fallacy.
questions.explanation: Ad hominem (Latin: 'to the person') attacks the speaker's character instead of refuting their argument. 'You can't trust King's views on segregation because he committed marital infidelities' is ad hominem. W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim handling; ad hominem is unfair. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays penalize this fallacy in student writing and may test recognition of it in sources. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score reasoning quality.

questions.prompt: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to refute commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Straw man fallacy distorts the opposing argument into a weaker version, then refutes the weaker version. 'Critics want zero technology in schools'—when critics actually want limits, not zero. W.9-10.1b requires fair counterclaim handling; straw man is unfair. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may test recognition in sources or penalize it in student work. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays reward fair representation.

questions.prompt: Assuming one event led to another simply because it happened first commits this Latin-named fallacy.
questions.explanation: Post hoc ergo propter hoc ('after this, therefore because of this') assumes sequence implies causation. 'Crime rose after the new mayor took office, so the mayor caused crime.' W.9-10.1b requires sound reasoning. RI.9-10.8 covers evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument essays explicitly require sound causal reasoning; the post hoc fallacy is one of the most commonly tested. Correlation does not equal causation.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'however,' 'on the other hand,' and 'critics argue' signal this shift in argument.
questions.explanation: Counterclaim transitions signal the shift from the writer's claim to the opposing view, then back. Common: 'however,' 'on the other hand,' 'critics argue,' 'while some maintain.' W.9-10.1c explicitly requires using 'words, phrases, and clauses to link the major sections of the text, create cohesion, and clarify the relationships between claim(s) and counterclaims.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward clear transition use. AP Lang scoring favors signposted argument structure.

questions.prompt: Citing sources within parentheses to maintain academic honesty is called this kind of attribution.
questions.explanation: In-text citation places source information in parentheses near the cited material. MLA, APA, and Chicago each have specific formats. For Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays, citations follow the numbered source format ('Source 1' or '(Source 1)'). W.9-10.8 covers citation. AP Lang requires source attribution in synthesis essays. Plagiarism prevention rests on consistent citation. Failure to cite drops Regents essay scores; AP Lang scores remain unaffected by format errors but lose ethos.

questions.prompt: Opening with a provocative question to engage readers' curiosity creates this hook type.
questions.explanation: Rhetorical questions don't expect literal answers; they engage readers in thinking. Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852) begins with this question and develops the answer. W.9-10.3a covers engaging openings; W.9-10.2a covers expository openings. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes use rhetorical questions. AP Lang argument and narrative essays accept rhetorical questions when purposeful, not gimmicky.

questions.prompt: Opening with a startling statistic or fact to seize attention uses this hook strategy.
questions.explanation: Statistic hooks (a.k.a. data openers) begin with a startling number to seize attention: 'Every twelve minutes, an American teen attempts suicide.' Effective in expository and argumentative writing when the data is credible and relevant. W.9-10.2a covers engaging openings for informational/explanatory texts. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept this opening. AP Lang argument essays reward credible statistic openings but penalize fabricated or irrelevant numbers.

questions.prompt: A narrative that hints at future events through suggestion uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Foreshadowing plants hints of future events to build suspense or thematic resonance. Romeo and Juliet's Prologue announces 'star-cross'd lovers' will die, then the entire play foreshadows the deaths. W.9-10.3c covers narrative techniques. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from recognizing foreshadowing in source texts. AP Lit narrative writing rewards strategic foreshadowing; analysis essays prize identification of foreshadowing's thematic effect.

questions.prompt: Defining a term by giving its category and distinguishing features is this kind of expository move.
questions.explanation: Classification (formal) definition places a term in its larger category (genus) and identifies distinguishing features (species/differentia). 'A sonnet (genus: poem) is a 14-line poem with specific rhyme schemes (species).' W.9-10.2b covers using definitions and other expository techniques. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing sometimes requires definition. AP Lang synthesis and rhetorical analysis benefit from precise definition. Aristotle's logic underlies this technique.

questions.prompt: Starting with a brief, vivid story to illustrate a larger point uses this opening technique.
questions.explanation: Anecdote opens an essay with a brief, vivid story illustrating a larger point. Effective in expository, argumentative, and narrative writing. W.9-10.3a covers narrative openings. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes accept anecdotal openings; AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays often praise them when the anecdote sets up the broader argument. Beware: anecdotes must support, not substitute for, broader evidence.

questions.prompt: Sensory details engage these five channels of perception in narrative writing.
questions.explanation: Effective narrative writing engages all five senses: sight, sound, smell, taste, touch. W.9-10.3d explicitly requires 'precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language to convey a vivid picture of the experiences, events, setting, and/or characters.' Beginning writers tend to over-rely on sight; smell and sound are underused and powerful. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards vivid prose. AP Lang and AP Lit reward sensory range as sophistication.

questions.prompt: Writing that demonstrates a character is angry through action rather than stating 'she was angry' uses this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Show, don't tell' is the foundational narrative principle: demonstrate emotion, character, and meaning through action, dialogue, and sensory detail rather than stating it directly. Instead of 'Scout was scared,' write 'Scout's hand tightened around Atticus's coat sleeve.' W.9-10.3d explicitly requires sensory language to convey experience. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards showing. AP Lit narrative scenes reward showing as a sophistication marker.

questions.prompt: Description that uses unexpected comparison, like 'her laugh sounded like breaking glass,' creates this device.
questions.explanation: Imagery uses sensory language and figurative comparison to create vivid mental pictures or sensations. Strong imagery often pairs sense with simile or metaphor: 'her laugh sounded like breaking glass.' W.9-10.3d requires sensory language. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards imagery in student responses. AP Lit narrative and rhetorical analysis essays both score imagery use. Distinguished from mere description, imagery does more than catalog; it interprets.

questions.prompt: Opening with a striking image, sound, or sensation that drops readers into the scene uses this technique.
