questions.explanation: Sensory hooks open with immediate sensory immersion: the heat of a Maycomb sidewalk, the salt-spray of Ship-Trap Island. W.9-10.3a explicitly requires 'engaging' narrative openings with 'precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards engagement. AP Lang narrative openings on the synthesis or argument essays often reward sensory immediacy. Strong sensory openings model the descriptive technique the writer will use throughout.

questions.prompt: Concluding an expository essay by restating the topic and explaining its broader significance uses this move.
questions.explanation: The 'so what' conclusion explains why the topic matters in a broader context beyond the essay itself. Weak conclusions paraphrase the thesis; strong conclusions extend its significance. W.9-10.2f explicitly requires a concluding statement that 'follows from and supports the information or explanation presented.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and Part 4 text-analysis writing both reward 'so what' conclusions. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays score sophistication via meaningful conclusions.

questions.prompt: The pattern of exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution is Freytag's this.
questions.explanation: Freytag's Pyramid (Gustav Freytag, 1863) maps the five-part dramatic structure: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution/denouement. It describes classical tragedy and most popular narrative. W.9-10.3a covers narrative organization. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards understanding of structure even when not strictly required. AP Lit narrative writing and analysis both require structural awareness. Modern narratives may invert or fragment the pyramid for effect.

questions.prompt: A narrative that returns to earlier events to fill in background uses this temporal device.
questions.explanation: Flashback (analepsis) interrupts present-tense narrative with scenes from earlier in time. Homer's Odyssey uses extensive flashback (Books 9-12 are Odysseus narrating his past wanderings). W.9-10.3c covers narrative techniques 'to develop experiences, events, and/or characters' and explicitly mentions 'pacing.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from structural sophistication. AP Lit narrative analysis frequently asks about temporal manipulation.

questions.prompt: Writing that explains how an event produces specific outcomes uses this organizational pattern.
questions.explanation: Cause-and-effect organization explains how events produce outcomes (or how outcomes follow from events). Can be single cause/multiple effects, multiple causes/single effect, or chains. W.9-10.2a covers organizing complex ideas. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing often analyzes cause-effect in source texts. AP Lang synthesis essays often hinge on causal reasoning. Avoid post hoc fallacies (mistaking sequence for causation).

questions.prompt: A narrative that resists chronological order to create thematic resonance uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Nonlinear narrative arranges events out of chronological order to create thematic or emotional effect. The Joy Luck Club, Beloved, and Slaughterhouse-Five all use nonlinear structure. W.9-10.3a covers organization choices; W.9-10.3c covers pacing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from recognizing structural sophistication. AP Lit narrative writing and analysis both reward purposeful nonlinearity. Distinct from flashback (which is a single jump back); nonlinearity restructures the whole.

questions.prompt: The rhythm and length pattern of sentences across a passage is called this.
questions.explanation: Sentence variety (syntactic diversity) mixes sentence lengths and structures: short punchy sentences for emphasis, longer flowing sentences for development, fragments for impact. Monotonous syntax bores readers and signals weak craft. W.9-10.5 covers revision; W.9-10.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards varied syntax in student responses. AP Lang and AP Lit narrative writing and analysis both prize syntactic range as a sophistication marker.

questions.prompt: Beginning a narrative or essay in the middle of action, like the Odyssey's opening, uses this Latin-named technique.
questions.explanation: In medias res (Latin: 'in the middle of things') begins narrative in mid-action, then fills in background. Homer's Odyssey opens with Odysseus stranded on Calypso's island, seven years into his wandering, then flashes back via his own narration. W.9-10.3a requires engaging openings. Regents Part 4 text-analysis response and AP Lang narrative-style argument writing both reward strong openings. The technique creates immediate stakes and pulls readers in.

questions.prompt: Writing that explains a process, defines a concept, or presents information without arguing is called this.
questions.explanation: Expository (explanatory) writing informs without persuading: how-to guides, definitions, classifications, cause-effect explanations, compare-contrast essays. W.9-10.2 explicitly covers expository writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing is partly expository (explaining the text). AP Lang has historically included synthesis essays, which are largely expository. Distinct from argumentative (which takes a position) and narrative (which tells a story); expository writing prioritizes clarity over claim.

questions.prompt: An expository essay comparing two texts is organized as either point-by-point or this.
questions.explanation: Block organization for compare-contrast presents all features of Subject A, then all features of Subject B (whole-to-whole), contrasted with point-by-point organization (which alternates: feature 1 in A and B, then feature 2 in A and B). W.9-10.2a explicitly covers organization for explanatory writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing accepts either structure. AP Lang synthesis essays reward purposeful organizational choice. Block works for brief contrasts; point-by-point for nuanced comparison.

questions.prompt: Writing that uses long, complex sentences with multiple clauses creates this stylistic effect.
questions.explanation: Periodic (hypotactic) style uses long, subordinated sentences with delayed main clauses, building momentum to a final point. Henry James, Faulkner, and Toni Morrison write in periodic styles. Contrast with paratactic (short, coordinate) style: Hemingway, Carver. W.9-10.4 covers style; W.9-10.5 covers revision. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from syntactic analysis. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward syntactic precision: not just 'long sentences,' but 'periodic.'.

questions.prompt: Using vivid verbs like 'creeped,' 'pounced,' or 'whispered' instead of 'walked,' 'caught,' or 'said' demonstrates this.
questions.explanation: Strong, specific verbs do more work than weak verbs propped up by adverbs. 'She walked angrily' is weaker than 'She stormed.' W.9-10.3d requires 'precise words and phrases.' W.9-10.2d covers precise language in informational writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards strong verb choice. AP Lit narrative scenes prize specific verbs. E.B. White's 'The Elements of Style' (1959) made strong verbs canonical.

questions.prompt: The distinctive way a writer expresses ideas, recognizable across their works, is called this.
questions.explanation: Voice is the writer's distinctive expression of personality through diction, syntax, and rhythm, recognizable across works. Toni Morrison's voice differs from Hemingway's. Beginning writers borrow voice; mature writers develop their own. W.9-10.3d covers narrative voice; W.9-10.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from voice analysis in source texts. AP Lang and AP Lit both heavily reward distinctive student voice.

questions.prompt: Hemingway's brief, declarative sentences without subordination create this contrasting stylistic effect.
questions.explanation: Paratactic style strings independent clauses without subordination, creating a clipped, declarative effect. Hemingway's 'In the late summer of that year we lived in a house in a village that looked across the river and the plain to the mountains' is a famous opening. W.9-10.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards style awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize precise syntactic naming (paratactic vs hypotactic). Useful for contrast analysis.

questions.prompt: The voice and consciousness through which a narrative is told is called this in fiction.
questions.explanation: Narrative perspective is the consciousness through which the story reaches the reader. Choices include first-person (I), second-person (you, rare), and third-person (limited, omniscient, or objective). W.9-10.3a requires establishing 'a point of view.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing requires perspective awareness in analysis. AP Lit narrative writing demands deliberate perspective choice; analysis essays often hinge on POV's effect on meaning.

questions.prompt: Replacing 'said' with 'whispered,' 'shouted,' or 'mumbled' affects this dimension of writing.
questions.explanation: Diction is word choice. Three levels: formal (academic), informal (conversational), colloquial (dialect, slang). Style-conscious writers vary diction strategically. W.9-10.5 covers revision for style; W.9-10.3d requires precise words. RL.9-10.4 covers how word choice shapes meaning. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both heavily score diction awareness: not just 'good word choice,' but precise diction categories and effect.

questions.prompt: The deliberate slowing of narrative time to emphasize a moment, like Lennie's death scene, uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Scene expansion stretches narrative time over a brief story moment to emphasize its weight. Steinbeck slows the final pages of Of Mice and Men around George shooting Lennie, lingering on the rabbit dream, the sunset, and George's calm voice. W.9-10.3c covers pacing 'to develop experiences, events, and/or characters.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from pacing awareness. AP Lit narrative scenes prize controlled pacing as sophistication.

questions.prompt: The comma before 'and' in 'red, white, and blue' is called this disputed punctuation.
questions.explanation: The Oxford (serial) comma precedes the final conjunction in a series. Strong arguments for using it: prevents ambiguity ('I dedicate this book to my parents, Ayn Rand and God' vs '...my parents, Ayn Rand, and God'). AP, MLA, and Chicago vary in requirement. L.9-10.2 covers conventions; Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing follows MLA/standard conventions. Most teachers favor Oxford comma for clarity. AP Lang penalizes mechanical errors only when they impede understanding.

questions.prompt: A comma splice incorrectly joins two of these in a single sentence.
questions.explanation: Comma splices join two independent clauses (each could stand alone) with only a comma, creating a punctuation error. Fix: add a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so), use a semicolon, or split into two sentences. L.9-10.2 explicitly covers conventions including punctuation. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes comma splices when they impede clarity. AP Lang scoring affects rhetorical effectiveness when errors mount.

questions.prompt: Commas around 'who lived in Maycomb' in 'Atticus, who lived in Maycomb, was a lawyer' mark this clause type.
questions.explanation: Nonrestrictive (non-essential) clauses add information that could be removed without changing the sentence's core meaning. They take commas. Compare restrictive: 'The lawyer who defended Tom Robinson was Atticus' (no commas; specifies which lawyer). L.9-10.2a covers semicolons and conventions; L.9-10.2b covers commas. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests recognition of restrictive vs nonrestrictive. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise punctuation. The 'who/which/that' distinction often hinges on this.

questions.prompt: Two complete sentences joined with only a comma create this specific punctuation error.
questions.explanation: A comma splice joins two independent clauses with only a comma. 'Atticus defended Tom Robinson, he was the only lawyer who would.' Fixes: 'Atticus defended Tom Robinson; he was the only lawyer who would' or 'Atticus defended Tom Robinson because he was the only lawyer who would.' L.9-10.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes comma splices in formal writing. AP Lang allows occasional intentional splices for stylistic effect (Cormac McCarthy), but unintentional ones lower writing quality.

questions.prompt: Beginning a sentence with 'After the war ended,' uses this kind of introductory element requiring a comma.
questions.explanation: Introductory adverbial phrases (prepositional, participial, infinitive) preceding the main clause take a comma. 'After the war ended, soldiers returned home.' 'Running through the woods, Rainsford spotted Zaroff.' L.9-10.2 explicitly covers punctuation conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both reward correct introductory comma usage. Failure to place introductory commas reads as basic-error and weakens credibility.

questions.prompt: A modifier that has no clear subject in the sentence has this name.
questions.explanation: A dangling modifier has no clear noun to modify, creating awkward or unintentionally funny sentences. 'Running through the woods, the tree branches scratched her' implies the branches were running. Fix: 'Running through the woods, she felt branches scratch her.' L.9-10.1 covers conventions; L.9-10.2 covers errors. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests dangling modifier recognition; AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays favor clear modifier placement.

questions.prompt: A modifier placed too far from the word it describes creates this kind of error.
questions.explanation: Misplaced modifiers sit too far from the words they modify, creating ambiguity. 'She almost drove for six hours' (=almost six hours, or almost drove?). Fix: 'She drove for almost six hours.' L.9-10.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests modifier placement. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays score writing clarity; misplaced modifiers impair clarity. Common with 'only,' 'just,' 'almost,' 'nearly'—place these directly before the word modified.

questions.prompt: An adjective like 'green' describes a noun; this part of speech modifies verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.
questions.explanation: Adverbs modify verbs (ran quickly), adjectives (very green), and other adverbs (extremely well). Most end in -ly but not all (fast, hard, well, soon). L.9-10.1 covers conventions; L.9-10.1a explicitly addresses 'various types of phrases and clauses' usage. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests grammatical accuracy. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays sometimes hinge on adverb effects (intensifiers, time/place markers). Distinguish adverb from adjective: 'She felt bad' (adjective; she is sad) vs 'She felt badly' (adverb; her sense of touch is impaired).

questions.prompt: A phrase like 'sitting on the porch' that functions as an adjective in 'the woman sitting on the porch' is called this.
questions.explanation: Participial phrases use a present (-ing) or past (-ed) participle plus modifiers to function as an adjective. 'Sitting on the porch, the woman watched her children.' L.9-10.1a covers 'various types of phrases.' L.9-10.1b covers 'parallel structure.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests sentence-level grammar. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize varied phrasal openings. Participial phrases create syntactic variety and embed information efficiently.

questions.prompt: A construction like 'to be or not to be' uses this kind of grammatical structure for emphasis.
questions.explanation: Parallel structure (parallelism) places equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms. Hamlet's 'To be, or not to be' parallels infinitive verbs. King's 'I have a dream that... I have a dream that...' is parallel. L.9-10.1b explicitly requires 'parallel structure.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes faulty parallelism. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays explicitly reward parallel structure identification in source texts. Faulty parallelism: 'I like swimming, running, and to bike.' Fix: 'swimming, running, and biking.'.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'bene' meaning 'good' appears in 'benevolent,' 'benefit,' and this word for advantage.
questions.explanation: The Latin root 'bene' (good) generates 'benevolent' (wishing well), 'benefit' (advantage), 'benefactor' (one who does good), 'benediction' (good speech, blessing). Common Core L.9-10.4b explicitly requires identifying and using common Latin and Greek roots. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice and Part 3 source-based argument essays test vocabulary built on roots. AP Lang and AP Lit both reward sophisticated vocabulary; roots enable inference of unfamiliar words. Contrast 'mal' (bad): malevolent, malefactor.

questions.prompt: The Greek root 'graph' meaning 'write' appears in 'autograph,' 'biography,' and this writing-related word.
questions.explanation: Greek root 'graph' (write) generates 'autograph' (self-writing), 'biography' (life-writing), 'photograph' (light-writing), 'cartography' (map-writing), 'calligraphy' (beautiful writing). Paired with 'photo' (light), 'auto' (self), 'bio' (life). L.9-10.4b requires root and affix analysis. Regents Part 2 vocabulary questions reward root knowledge. AP Lang and AP Lit assume students can decode unfamiliar words via roots. Greek roots dominate scientific vocabulary; Latin roots dominate legal and academic vocabulary.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'spec' or 'spect' meaning 'look' appears in 'inspect,' 'spectacle,' and this prefixed word.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'spec/spect' (look, see) generates 'inspect' (look into), 'spectator' (one who looks), 'spectacular' (worth looking at), 'speculate' (look from above, theorize), 'perspective' (look through), 'suspect' (look from beneath, doubt). L.9-10.4b requires roots and affixes. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests root recognition. AP Lang and AP Lit assume root literacy. Note Latin morphology: prefix + root + suffix yields meaning through composition.

questions.prompt: The Greek root 'demos' meaning 'people' appears in 'democracy,' 'pandemic,' and this scholar.
questions.explanation: Greek root 'demos' (people) generates 'democracy' (rule by the people), 'demographer' (people-writer, population scientist), 'demagogue' (people-leader, populist orator), 'endemic' (in the people, native), 'epidemic' (upon the people), 'pandemic' (all the people). L.9-10.4b covers roots. Regents Part 2 vocabulary and Part 3 source-based argument both reward root knowledge. AP Lang civics texts often use these roots. Plato's 'Republic' critiques 'demos' rule; Madison's Federalist No. 10 distinguishes democracy from republic.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'dict' meaning 'say' or 'speak' appears in 'dictate,' 'predict,' and this 'banned word' term.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'dict' (say, speak) generates 'dictate' (speak forth), 'predict' (speak before), 'contradict' (speak against), 'verdict' (truly spoken, judgment), 'edict' (proclamation), 'diction' (manner of speaking), 'interdict' (speak between, forbid). L.9-10.4b explicitly covers Latin and Greek roots. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests these. AP Lang argument essays rely on precise diction; understanding 'dict' family deepens word-precision skill. 'Benediction' (good speech) ties this category back to category 1.

questions.prompt: Calling a king 'the crown' or a CEO 'the suit' uses this device where a part stands for the whole.
questions.explanation: Synecdoche substitutes a part for the whole (or vice versa). 'All hands on deck' uses 'hands' for sailors; 'the crown' uses a crown for monarchy. L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test synecdoche; AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward identification. Often confused with metonymy (related but separate concept). Modern: 'the Pentagon decided' (the building for the military leadership).

questions.prompt: Calling the U.S. president 'the White House' uses this related device of substitution by association.
questions.explanation: Metonymy substitutes a closely associated thing for another: 'the White House' for the U.S. president, 'Wall Street' for the financial industry, 'the pen is mightier than the sword' (pen for writing, sword for violence). L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. Distinguished from synecdoche (which uses part-whole specifically) by being broader (association of any kind). Regents Part 2 vocabulary may test metonymy. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward precise device-naming. Difficult: synecdoche may technically be a type of metonymy.

questions.prompt: Combining contradictory terms like 'jumbo shrimp' or 'deafening silence' creates this device.
questions.explanation: Oxymoron pairs contradictory terms for striking effect: 'jumbo shrimp,' 'bittersweet,' 'living dead,' 'deafening silence.' From Greek 'oxys' (sharp) + 'moros' (dull). Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet builds an oxymoron catalogue at 1.1.169-178: 'O brawling love, O loving hate.' L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests oxymoron recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward precise identification. Often confused with paradox (which is a longer statement, not a phrase).

questions.prompt: A statement that seems contradictory but reveals truth, like 'less is more,' is this device.
questions.explanation: A paradox is a statement that appears self-contradictory but reveals deeper truth. 'Less is more' (more meaning by saying less). 'I must be cruel only to be kind' (Hamlet). Distinguished from oxymoron (which is a phrase) and contradiction (which has no truth). L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes hinge on paradoxical claims. AP Lang and AP Lit reward paradox recognition. Religious paradox: 'whoever loses his life will find it.'.

questions.prompt: Saying 'I have a dream' just after 'I am not unmindful' uses this device of negative assertion.
questions.explanation: Litotes (LIE-toh-teez) understates by negating the opposite: 'not bad' for 'good,' 'no small problem' for 'a big problem,' 'I am not unmindful' for 'I am mindful.' L.9-10.5a covers figurative language. MLK's 'I am not unmindful that some of you have come here out of great trials and tribulations' uses litotes to emphasize awareness. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice rarely tests litotes; AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize identification. Anglo-Saxon literature (Beowulf) heavily uses litotes.

questions.prompt: Determining a word's meaning from surrounding text and sentence structure uses this strategy.
questions.explanation: Context clues are surrounding text that helps infer a word's meaning: definition clues, example clues, contrast clues, restatement clues. L.9-10.4a explicitly requires 'use context (e.g., the overall meaning of a sentence, paragraph, or text; a word's position or function in a sentence) as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase.' Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests vocabulary in context frequently. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays assume context-clue mastery. Skill is foundational for unfamiliar texts.

questions.prompt: A word's literal dictionary definition is called this.
questions.explanation: Denotation is a word's literal, dictionary definition. Contrasts with connotation (associations, emotional/cultural baggage). 'House' and 'home' have similar denotations but different connotations. L.9-10.4 covers vocabulary; L.9-10.5b explicitly distinguishes 'connotations (associations) of words with similar denotations (definitions).' Regents Part 2 multiple-choice often tests denotation/connotation distinction. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must distinguish these for precise word-choice analysis. Foundational vocabulary distinction.

questions.prompt: The emotional or cultural associations a word carries beyond its dictionary meaning are this.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the cluster of associations, emotions, and cultural baggage a word carries. 'Childish' and 'childlike' share denotation (relating to children) but opposite connotations (negative vs positive). L.9-10.5b explicitly covers connotation/denotation. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests connotative awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must analyze connotation as part of diction analysis. Mastery requires extensive reading; connotations shift across cultures and eras.

questions.prompt: Words sharing the same essential meaning, like 'big' and 'enormous,' are called these.
questions.explanation: Synonyms share core meaning but typically differ in connotation, formality, or precision. 'Big,' 'large,' 'enormous,' 'colossal' progress in intensity. L.9-10.6 covers vocabulary acquisition. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests synonym selection in context. AP Lang and AP Lit reward precise word selection; true synonyms are rare, and choosing among synonyms is the craft of writing. Roget's Thesaurus warns against treating synonyms as interchangeable.

questions.prompt: Words from one language that have entered another, like 'kindergarten' or 'safari,' are called these.
questions.explanation: Loanwords are borrowings from one language into another. English has absorbed 'kindergarten' (German), 'safari' (Swahili), 'sushi' (Japanese), 'siesta' (Spanish). L.9-10.4 covers vocabulary; L.9-10.6 covers acquiring and using accurate words. English's loanword saturation (over half its vocabulary is borrowed, mostly French/Latin/Greek) makes etymology critical. Regents Part 2 vocabulary may test borrowed-word meaning. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays often reward etymological awareness when discussing diction.

questions.prompt: Harper Lee's 1960 novel narrated by young Scout Finch about her father's defense of Tom Robinson.
questions.explanation: Harper Lee's To Kill a Mockingbird (1960) won the 1961 Pulitzer Prize. Narrated by adult Scout looking back on her 6-9-year-old experiences, it explores Maycomb's racism through Atticus Finch's defense of Tom Robinson. RL.9-10.2 and RL.9-10.3 both can be assessed via TKAM. Common Core lists it among the exemplar texts for Grades 9-10. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on prejudice, courage, and empathy frequently use this novel.

questions.prompt: George Orwell's allegorical novella about a barnyard revolution that mirrors the Russian Revolution.
questions.explanation: George Orwell's Animal Farm (1945) satirizes the Russian Revolution: Old Major (Marx/Lenin), Napoleon (Stalin), Snowball (Trotsky), Boxer (working class). The seven commandments shrink to one. RL.9-10.2 covers theme; RL.9-10.5 covers structure. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on power, propaganda, or revolution often draw on Animal Farm. AP Lang argument essays may pair it with primary documents on Stalin.

questions.prompt: Hints of future events scattered in earlier text, like the Prologue of Romeo and Juliet.
questions.explanation: Foreshadowing plants hints of future events to build suspense or thematic resonance. The Prologue of Romeo and Juliet announces 'star-cross'd lovers' will die. RL.9-10.5 covers how authors structure texts. W.9-10.3c covers narrative techniques. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests foreshadowing recognition. AP Lit narrative writing rewards strategic foreshadowing; analysis essays prize identifying foreshadowing's effect. Distinct from prophecy (which announces) and from clear prediction (which removes suspense).

questions.prompt: Restating an author's idea in your own words while preserving meaning is called this.
questions.explanation: Paraphrase restates an author's idea in your own words with attribution. Distinct from summary (compression) and quotation (verbatim). W.9-10.8 covers paraphrasing source material. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept paraphrase with attribution. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays reward strategic mixing of quotation and paraphrase. Plagiarism warning: paraphrasing without attribution counts as plagiarism. Effective paraphrase requires reordering syntax, not just synonym substitution.

questions.prompt: Irony when the audience knows something a character does not, used heavily in Romeo and Juliet.
questions.explanation: Dramatic irony occurs when the audience knows something a character does not, creating tension between knowledge levels. Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet ending (audience knows Juliet is alive when Romeo drinks poison) is canonical. RL.9-10.5 covers how authors structure texts for effect. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests irony types. AP Lit narrative analysis demands precise irony naming: verbal (saying opposite), situational (event-outcome mismatch), dramatic (audience-character gap).

questions.prompt: A direct comparison without 'like' or 'as,' like 'Juliet is the sun.'.
questions.explanation: A metaphor equates two unlike things to suggest shared qualities, without 'like' or 'as.' Romeo's 'Juliet is the sun' compresses comparison. RL.9-10.4 explicitly covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests metaphor identification; Part 3 source-based arguments often analyze metaphor's role in developing theme. AP Lang and AP Lit demand precise metaphor-effect analysis. Extended metaphors (conceits) develop across passages; metaphors can be implied or explicit.

questions.prompt: Homer's epic following a Greek king's ten-year voyage home from Troy.
questions.explanation: Homer's Odyssey (c. 8th century BCE) follows Odysseus's ten-year return from Troy through encounters with Polyphemus, Circe, Calypso, and the underworld. The hero's journey, themes of nostos (homecoming), and the Telemachy frame archetypal narrative. RL.9-10.2 covers theme; RL.9-10.9 covers Greek mythology's influence on later literature. Common Core lists The Odyssey for Grades 9-10. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on perseverance often draw on Homer.

questions.prompt: Richard Connell's 1924 short story about Rainsford hunted by General Zaroff on a remote island.
questions.explanation: Richard Connell's 'The Most Dangerous Game' (1924) inverts hunter-prey hierarchies as American hunter Rainsford finds himself hunted by Russian aristocrat Zaroff on Ship-Trap Island. The story's ending ('He had never slept in a better bed') resolves the moral inversion. RL.9-10.2 covers theme; RL.9-10.3 covers character. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on ethics or survival often use this classic short story.

questions.prompt: An extended narrative where characters and events systematically represent abstract ideas, like Animal Farm.
questions.explanation: Allegory sustains a narrative in which characters, events, and settings systematically represent abstract ideas or specific historical referents. Animal Farm allegorizes the Russian Revolution; The Pilgrim's Progress allegorizes Christian salvation. RL.9-10.4 covers figurative meaning; RL.9-10.9 covers transformation of source material. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on power and propaganda often demand allegorical reading. AP Lit treats allegory as a structural and thematic device.

questions.prompt: A character whose contrast highlights another character's traits, like Mercutio to Romeo.
questions.explanation: A foil is a character whose traits contrast with another's to illuminate that character. Mercutio's cynicism foils Romeo's idealism; Lennie's innocence foils George's worldliness; Snowball's idealism foils Napoleon's ruthlessness. RL.9-10.3 covers character analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on characterization often analyze foils. AP Lit analysis essays prize precise foil identification and effect explanation: not 'X is a foil to Y,' but 'how X's foil illuminates Y's specific traits.'.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's three rhetorical appeals are ethos, pathos, and this third Greek term.
questions.explanation: Aristotle's three artistic proofs are ethos (speaker's credibility), pathos (emotional appeal), and logos (logical appeal through evidence and reasoning). RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both require Aristotelian appeal identification. Strong rhetoric balances all three; over-reliance on pathos (manipulative) or logos (cold) weakens persuasion. Knowing Aristotle's framework unlocks any speech analysis.

questions.prompt: Repeating a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses, like MLK's 'I have a dream,' creates this.
questions.explanation: Anaphora repeats a word or phrase at the start of successive clauses. King's eight 'I have a dream' repetitions drive momentum. Lincoln's 'we cannot dedicate, we cannot consecrate, we cannot hallow' uses anaphora. RI.9-10.6 covers rhetoric; L.9-10.5 covers figurative language. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward anaphora identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise device-naming. Distinguish anaphora (beginning repetition) from epistrophe (ending repetition).

questions.prompt: Logical errors like ad hominem and straw man are collectively called these.
questions.explanation: Logical fallacies are reasoning errors that may seem persuasive but fail logically. Common: ad hominem (attacking the person), straw man (misrepresenting opposition), post hoc (assuming temporal sequence implies causation), hasty generalization (insufficient evidence), false dilemma (only two options), slippery slope (chain of negative consequences). RI.9-10.8 explicitly requires evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward fallacy identification.

questions.prompt: MLK's 1963 letter, written from a Birmingham jail cell, responding to white clergy critics.
questions.explanation: King's Letter from Birmingham Jail (April 16, 1963) responds to eight Alabama clergymen's open letter calling his protests 'unwise and untimely.' King's letter cites Aquinas, Augustine, Buber, and the prophets to justify civil disobedience. Common Core lists it as a founding informational text for Grades 9-10. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view; RI.9-10.8 covers argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on justice routinely use this letter. AP Lang frequently features it.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's framework of writer, audience, and subject forms this rhetorical figure.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical triangle (Aristotle) connects writer/speaker (ethos), audience (pathos), and subject (logos). Effective rhetoric balances all three angles. RI.9-10.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require triangle awareness. AP Lang's rhetorical analysis essay is built on this framework. Mapping the triangle answers: who is speaking, to whom, about what, and how the writer's choices serve all three points.

questions.prompt: The author's specific, arguable position in an essay is called this.
questions.explanation: A thesis is the writer's specific, arguable, defensible position. W.9-10.1a requires precise claims. Strong theses are debatable (not just factual), specific (not vague), and forecast organization. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score thesis quality heavily. AP Lang argument essays require thesis identification and defense. The thesis controls organization: every paragraph supports it. Beginning writers confuse thesis with topic; precise thesis differentiation distinguishes strong from weak writing.

questions.prompt: Demonstrating a character is angry through action rather than telling readers uses this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Show, don't tell' demands writers demonstrate emotion, character, and meaning through action, dialogue, and sensory detail rather than stating it directly. 'Scout's hand tightened around Atticus's coat sleeve' shows fear; 'Scout was scared' tells. W.9-10.3d requires sensory language to convey experience. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards showing. AP Lit narrative writing scores showing heavily. Foundational rule of fiction craft, attributed variously to Henry James and Anton Chekhov.

questions.prompt: Placing equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms, like 'I came, I saw, I conquered,' creates this.
questions.explanation: Parallel structure (parallelism) places equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms. Caesar's 'Veni, vidi, vici' (I came, I saw, I conquered) is canonical. L.9-10.1b explicitly requires parallel structure. Faulty parallelism: 'I like swimming, running, and to bike.' Fix: 'swimming, running, and biking.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests parallelism. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward parallelism identification in source texts. Hallmark of polished prose.

questions.prompt: The opposing view a writer must acknowledge and respond to in argumentative writing is this.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is the opposing position a writer addresses to strengthen the main claim. W.9-10.1b explicitly requires developing 'claim(s) and counterclaims fairly.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays evaluate counterclaim acknowledgment. The strongest arguments concede something to the counterclaim before refuting it. AP Lang argument essays heavily reward genuine counterclaim engagement. Refusing counterclaims signals weak argument and ego-driven writing.

questions.prompt: The speed and rhythm of narrative time, slowed for emphasis or quickened for summary, is called this.
questions.explanation: Pacing is the speed of narrative time relative to story time. Writers slow pace through scene, dialogue, and description; quicken through summary and ellipsis. W.9-10.3c explicitly requires pacing as a narrative technique. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards pacing awareness in source texts. AP Lit narrative writing prizes deliberate pacing; analysis essays often hinge on how pacing develops character or theme. Strong pacing creates rhythm and emphasizes key moments.

questions.prompt: Two complete sentences joined with only a comma create this specific punctuation error.
questions.explanation: A comma splice joins two independent clauses with only a comma. Fixes: add a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS: for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so), use a semicolon, or split into two sentences. L.9-10.2 explicitly covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes comma splices when they impede clarity. AP Lang scoring affects rhetorical effectiveness when errors mount. Distinct from run-on sentences (which lack any punctuation between clauses).

questions.prompt: The emotional and cultural associations of a word, beyond its literal meaning, are called this.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the cluster of associations, emotions, and cultural baggage a word carries. 'Childish' and 'childlike' share denotation (relating to children) but opposite connotations (negative vs positive). L.9-10.5b explicitly covers connotation/denotation. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests connotative awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand connotation analysis as part of diction. Mastery requires extensive reading; connotations shift across cultures and eras.

questions.prompt: The deliberate arrangement of words for effect, including sentence length and structure, is called this.
questions.explanation: Syntax is the arrangement of words in sentences, including order, length, and clause patterns. Periodic syntax (long, delayed main clause) builds suspense; paratactic syntax (short, declarative) creates impact. L.9-10.1 covers conventions; L.9-10.3 covers language knowledge. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards syntactic awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise syntax-effect analysis. Syntax differs from diction (word choice) and semantics (meaning); syntax is structure.

questions.prompt: A modifier without a clear subject in the sentence, like 'Running through the woods, the branches scratched her,' is this.
questions.explanation: A dangling modifier has no clear noun to modify, creating awkward or unintentionally funny sentences. 'Running through the woods, the tree branches scratched her' implies branches were running. Fix: 'Running through the woods, she felt branches scratch her.' L.9-10.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests dangling modifier recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays favor clear modifier placement. Often happens with participial-phrase openings.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'spec' or 'spect' meaning 'look' appears in 'inspect,' 'spectator,' and this term for theory.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'spec/spect' (look, see) generates 'inspect' (look into), 'spectator' (one who looks), 'speculate' (look from above, theorize), 'perspective' (look through), 'suspect' (look from beneath, doubt). L.9-10.4b explicitly requires roots and affixes. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests these. AP Lang and AP Lit reward root literacy as foundation for vocabulary inference. Latin morphology: prefix + root + suffix yields meaning through composition. Family also includes 'specimen,' 'specter,' 'aspect.'.
